exercises.title: Pomodoro Sprint Week
exercises.description: Complete one full work week using the Pomodoro Technique. Track every session in a simple log: task name, duration, interruptions, and completion status. At the end of the week, calculate your average pomodoros per day and identify which tasks work best with 25-minute sessions versus longer blocks.
hints: Use Pomofocus.io or a simple spreadsheet to track
Be honest about interruptions — log them even if you 'recovered'
Note which tasks you consistently finish within 25 minutes vs. which need multiple sessions

solution.code:
POMODORO TRACKING LOG:

Monday:
1. Code review (PR #142) - 25 min ✓ (1 pomodoro)
2. Fix auth bug - 50 min ✓ (2 pomodoros) 
3. Write API docs - 25 min - (interrupted at 15 min)
4. Refactor user module - 75 min ✓ (3 pomodoros)
5. Team standup - 15 min (not a pomodoro)
6. Email + Slack - 25 min (1 pomodoro)
Total: 7 pomodoros, 1 interrupted

Tuesday:
[Continue tracking...]

WEEKLY SUMMARY:
• Total pomodoros completed: 38
• Average per day: 7.6
• Most productive: Tuesday (10 pomodoros)
• Least productive: Wednesday (5 pomodoros, long meetings)
• Best for 25-min: Code reviews, documentation, small fixes
• Best for 50-min: Feature development, debugging
• Top interruption source: Slack DMs (42% of interruptions)

exercises.title: Interruption Defense System
exercises.description: Design and implement a personal interruption defense system. Create a set of Slack statuses, email auto-responders, and physical signals (headphones, desk signs) that protect your pomodoro time. Test them for one week and track which ones actually reduce interruptions.
hints: Prepare your defense BEFORE starting your first pomodoro
Test different Slack statuses and see which get the most respect
Physical signals (headphones) work surprisingly well for in-office interruptions

solution.code:
INTERRUPTION DEFENSE TOOLKIT:

SLACK STATUSES (rotate these):
• '🍅 In focus mode — back at [time]'
• '⚡ Deep work — DM me if urgent, otherwise I'll respond at break'
• '🔒 Heads down on [task] — will check Slack at [time]'

EMAIL AUTO-RESPONSE (for deep work hours):
'Thanks for your email. I check email at 11 AM and 4 PM daily. If this is urgent, please call me at [number].'

PHYSICAL SIGNALS:
• Headphones ON = do not disturb (even if not playing music)
• Desk sign: 'Focus block in progress — questions welcome after 2 PM'
• Second monitor OFF during pomodoros (reduces visual distraction)

WEBSITE BLOCKERS:
• Cold Turkey: Block social media, news, and non-work sites during pomodoros
• Freedom: Cross-device blocking (phone + laptop)
• Forest: Gamified focus — grow a virtual tree while you work

RESULTS TRACKER:
| Week | Avg interruptions/day | Defense used | Improvement |
| 1    | 8.2                   | None         | Baseline    |
| 2    | 5.1                   | Slack status | -38%        |
| 3    | 2.8                   | All tools    | -66%        |

exercises.title: Flow State Experiment
exercises.description: Run a controlled experiment to find your optimal pomodoro duration. Week 1: Use 25/5 (classic). Week 2: Use 50/10 (extended). Week 3: Use 90/20 (deep). Track your output quality and quantity for each duration. Determine which works best for different task types.
hints: Keep all other variables constant (same tasks, same time of day, same environment)
Measure both QUANTITY (tasks completed) and QUALITY (bugs found in review, satisfaction)
Your optimal duration may differ for different task types — that's valuable data

solution.code:
FLOW STATE EXPERIMENT RESULTS:

WEEK 1 - 25/5 Pomodoros:
• Pomodoros completed: 42
• Tasks completed: 18 (small-medium)
• Deep work hours: 10.5
• Quality score: 7/10 (some half-finished context switches)
• Notes: Great for code reviews and docs, frustrating for debugging

WEEK 2 - 50/10 Pomodoros:
• Pomodoros completed: 28
• Tasks completed: 12 (medium-large)
• Deep work hours: 14
• Quality score: 8/10 (better flow, fewer context switches)
• Notes: Sweet spot for feature development, still good for reviews

WEEK 3 - 90/20 Pomodoros:
• Pomodoros completed: 14
• Tasks completed: 6 (large, complex)
• Deep work hours: 14
• Quality score: 9/10 (deepest focus, best code quality)
• Notes: Best for architecture and complex debugging, exhausting

CONCLUSION:
• Code reviews + docs → 25/5
• Feature development → 50/10
• Architecture + complex debugging → 90/20
• Recommended: Mix 50/10 and 90/20 based on task complexity
